channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Follows His Gut. His National Security Advisers Try to Keep Up.
item.description: Decisions come fast, even if contradictions and inconsistencies abound. But without much of a process, there is little preparation for how things can go wrong.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump in the Oval Office on Tuesday. His decision to attack Iran, he said, was based on gut instinct.

item.title: Iranian TV and Social Media Project Defiant and Distorted View of the War
item.description: State media and online propagandists are striking a confident posture, despite heavy losses. Some of the content was generated by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Hsu, Steven Lee Myers and Stuart A. Thompson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: James Talarico Says ‘New Politics Is Being Born’ After Texas Primary Win
item.description: After clinching the Democratic Senate primary, he told supporters in Austin that the country’s real divide is between “top versus bottom,” not the left versus right.
item.dc:creator: Ramón Ramirez
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico speaking to supporters in Austin, Texas, on Wednesday night.

item.title: Daines Drops Re-election Bid in Montana, Upending a Senate Race
item.description: The surprise announcement by Senator Steve Daines could give Democrats a slim opening in the Republican-leaning state in their uphill fight to take the Senate majority.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Eric Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Steve Daines said it was time for new leaders from Montana and endorsed a Republican successor.

item.title: Aaron Spencer, Charged With Murder, Appears to Win Republican Nomination for County Sheriff in Arkansas
item.description: Aaron Spencer faces a second-degree murder charge for shooting a man accused of sexually assaulting his daughter. Preliminary results showed him appearing to win the Republican primary.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:description: A screengrab from a 2024 video by Aaron Spencer announcing his candidacy for sheriff of Lonoke County, Ark.

item.title: In a Riskier Era, China Bets on Technology to Resist U.S. Pressure
item.description: China announced a 7 percent increase in military spending and a five-year plan to try to reduce its military and industry’s reliance on Western technology.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Lily Kuo
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Lintao Zhang/Getty Images
item.media:description: Xi Jinping, China’s leader, at the annual meeting of the national legislature in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: China Sets Economy’s Growth Target Below 5% for First Time in Decades
item.description: The goal, announced at a gathering of Communist Party leaders, was the lowest since 1991 and can offer clues about China’s policymaking plans.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Krolik
item.media:credit: Florence Lo/Reuters
item.media:description: China’s leader, Xi Jinping, at the Great Hall of the People in Beijing on Wednesday.

item.title: Did You Know These Oscar-Nominated Actresses Started in Reality TV?
item.description: Hollywood careers don’t always begin where you expect them to, as Jessie Buckley, Teyana Taylor and Emma Stone could tell you.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan

item.title: Gonzales Admits Affair as Republicans Move to Censure Him
item.description: The day after being forced into a runoff to keep his seat, Representative Tony Gonzales confirmed that he had an extramarital affair with an aide who later took her own life.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Tony Gonzales, Republican of Texas, could be the latest House member censured for wrongdoing.

item.title: Justice Dept., Under Pressure From Trump, Fails to Build Autopen Case Against Biden
item.description: Prosecutors in the U.S. attorney’s office in Washington were unable to build a case, underscoring the department’s increasing inability to follow through on the president’s desire to indict his rivals.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt, Devlin Barrett and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Former President Joseph R. Biden Jr. has forcefully denied assertions that his use of the autopen broke the law, calling President Trump and his allies “liars.”

item.title: Noem and D.H.S. Inspector General Spar Over Obstruction Claim
item.description: Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem pushed back against her department’s inspector general, who told Congress in a letter that she had “systematically obstructed” his office’s work.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian, Hamed Aleaziz and Michael Gold
item.category: Homeland Security Office of Inspector General
item.media:credit: Kevin Dietsch/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Homeland Security Department’s inspector general, Joseph V. Cuffari, testifying last year. Mr. Cuffari has opened investigations that include an audit of how Immigration and Customs Enforcement investigates use-of-force incidents.

item.title: Contract for El Paso ICE Center Is Under Review, Homeland Security Says
item.description: Camp East Montana in Texas has been under growing scrutiny over its living conditions and has been grappling with a measles outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra and Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Camp East Montana, a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement detention facility at Fort Bliss in El Paso.

item.title: Several of Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer’s employees are under investigation for official misconduct under her leadership.
item.description: Several of Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer’s employees are under investigation for official misconduct under her leadership.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: An aide to Labor Secretary Lori Chavez-DeRemer was placed on administrative leave Wednesday.

item.title: Australia’s Sydney Jewish Museum to Memorialize Bondi Beach Massacre
item.description: The country and its small Jewish community are still trying to process the mass shooting at Bondi Beach in December.
item.dc:creator: Laura Chung and Matthew Abbott
item.media:credit: Matthew Abbott for The New York Times

item.title: Insulting the Mayor Is Nothing New. But This Was Shocking.
item.description: A well-known conservative radio host in New York called Mayor Zohran Mamdani a cockroach, resurfacing anti-Muslim sentiments that the mayor said were all too common.
item.dc:creator: Maya King and Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Adam Gray for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani of New York said that bigotry against Muslims is often greeted by silence that “allows it to fester.”

item.title: Most Patients Keep Weight Off With Fewer GLP-1 Shots, Study Finds
item.description: In a recent review, a small group of patients received the injections less frequently but still maintained weight loss and health benefits.
item.dc:creator: Roni Caryn Rabin
item.media:credit: Kristian Thacker for The New York Times

item.title: Lou Holtz, Who Coached Unbeaten Notre Dame to a Title, Dies at 89
item.description: Known for reviving football programs, he led six major colleges to bowl games, winning a national championship in 1989 after restoring the Irish to greatness.
item.dc:creator: Richard Goldstein
item.media:credit: Focus on Sport/Getty Images
item.media:description: Lou Holtz with Notre Dame players in about 1988. Unassuming in demeanor, he had a keen football mind and a disciplinarian’s resolve.

item.title: Tree Rings Reveal Origins of Some of the World’s Best Violins
item.description: A study of over 275 violins suggests that Antonio Stradivari used wood from high-altitude forests in northern Italy to craft some of his most renowned instruments.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei

item.title: After ‘Priceless’ Violin Flies on Lap, Lufthansa Changes Carry-On Rules
item.description: Carolin Widmann had to cradle her centuries-old, multimillion-dollar instrument during a flight to Germany. Her predicament resonated with musicians who have faced similar challenges.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: Brill/ullstein bild, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Carolin Widmann was boarding a plane with a Guadagnini violin, made in 1782, when she was instructed to take it out of its case.

item.title: Talarico Can Win. But He Will Not Turn Texas Blue.
item.description: Texas politics is in constant churn.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Hooks and John Guida
item.media:credit: Brenda Bazán/Associated Press

item.title: Trump Is Playing a Dangerous Game With Iran
item.description: Hungary. Cuba. Afghanistan. Iraq. Now Iran?
item.dc:creator: Peter W. Klein
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Naila Ruechel for The New York Times

item.title: What Are Your Prom Dinner Plans? We Want to Hear From You.
item.description: We may want to cover your big night for a story about high school’s most important event.
item.dc:creator: Priya Krishna
item.media:credit: Gillian Jones/The Berkshire Eagle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prom night is a time-honored American tradition. We’d like to understand it better.

item.title: Man Killed After Fleeing Texas Border Patrol Checkpoint, Police Say
item.description: The man fired shots at law enforcement and civilian vehicles before he was killed during a pursuit along Interstate 10 in West Texas.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Herika Martinez/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Border Patrol checkpoint in Sierra Blanca, Texas, last year.

item.title: Judge Blocks DeSantis’s Declaration of Muslim Group as Terrorist Organization
item.description: A federal judge found that the designation for CAIR, one of the nation’s largest Muslim advocacy groups, had violated the organization’s First Amendment rights.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Ron DeSantis of Florida signed an executive order in December declaring the Council on American-Islamic Relations a terrorist group.

item.title: Why Ecuador Invited the U.S. Military to Help With Its Drug Gangs
item.description: Drug gangs have turned the South American country into one of the most dangerous in the region and the world’s leading exporter of cocaine.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní, Eric Schmitt and José María León Cabrera
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Daniele Volpe for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Ecuadorian Coast Guard patrolling in 2025. The U.S. military is now helping Ecuador plan a series of raids targeting the South American country’s violent drug gangs.

item.title: Former Barclays Center Executive Says Live Nation Threatened to Pull Tours
item.description: The Justice Department had John Abbamondi testify at an antitrust trial to support its case that Live Nation has acted as a monopoly, an accusation the company denies.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Barclays Center in Brooklyn was the subject of heated testimony at the Live Nation trial on Wednesday.
